reviewed_map.rationale: These providers deliver agentic AI functionality primarily through hosted SaaS or managed cloud services, emphasizing scalability, managed infrastructure, and easy integration, which aligns with a cloud deployment model.
citations: Latent Space: The Rise of Agentic AI Platforms in 2025
TechCrunch: Agentic AI Funding Surge in Early 2026

reviewed_map.rationale: These vendors explicitly promote private, on-premises, or dedicated deployments, offering full control over data and infrastructure for enterprises that require self-hosted solutions.

retrieved_docs.title: Tavily — about
retrieved_docs.snippet:
Tavily — about

/about us
The agentic web is vast and unstructured. Tavily delivers high-quality, fresh, and structured web context, making it easy for agents to stay grounded as they scale from prototypes to production.
/our mission
Our mission is to help AI agents navigate the web quickly and efficiently by shaping the context they see. We prioritize relevance, freshness, and efficiency so agents reason accurately without wasting tokens.
/team
We’re a team of collaborators, builders, and deep thinkers, obsessed with making reliable web access possible for AI agents in the real world. We move quickly, support each other, and stay focused on shipping systems that deliver real impact in production.

retrieved_docs.title: Latent Space: The Rise of Agentic AI Platforms in 2025
retrieved_docs.snippet:
Latent Space: The Rise of Agentic AI Platforms in 2025

The Latent Space newsletter surveys the agentic AI platform landscape as of mid-2025. The analysis identifies three dominant approaches: (1) orchestration frameworks (CrewAI, AutoGen, LangGraph) focused on multi-agent coordination; (2) RAG-first platforms (LlamaIndex, Vertex AI Search) focused on grounding agents in enterprise data; and (3) evaluation-native platforms (Langfuse, Braintrust, PromptLayer) focused on reliability and measurement. The newsletter argues that evaluation will become the key differentiator as models commoditize.

retrieved_docs.title: YC W25 Request for Startups: AI Infrastructure
retrieved_docs.snippet:
YC W25 Request for Startups: AI Infrastructure

Y Combinator's Winter 2025 Request for Startups highlights AI infrastructure as a top investment thesis. Key areas include: developer tools for agentic AI (debugging, testing, deployment), evaluation frameworks for LLM reliability, multi-modal data pipelines, and cost-optimization layers for large language model inference. YC notes that startups solving agent observability and grounding are especially well-positioned.

retrieved_docs.snippet:
Cognition Blog — 1

Cognition is expanding its APAC presence from Singapore, where enterprise demand, developer talent, and national AI adoption are converging. With Richard Spence leading the region, we’re helping teams across Asia-Pacific bring autonomous software engineering into production.
Mercedes-Benz is deploying Devin and Windsurf across its global engineering organization, starting with legacy modernization, cloud-native development, and logistics.
Start a Devin session in your terminal. When the work outgrows your laptop, hand the same session off to the cloud and keep going.
Building cloud agents is difficult. We share some learnings from building Devin.
10 months ago I argued against building multi-agent systems. Today, a narrower class works, where agents contribute intelligence while writes stay single-threaded. I want to share what we've learned building them at Cognition.
Local agents make you faster. Cloud agents do the work while you're not there.
Smaller specialized models can match or beat frontier generalists on the tasks they're trained for. Working with Applied Compute, we RL-trained SWE-check, a bug detection model that matches Opus 4.6 on our internal evals while running ~10x

retrieved_docs.title: TechCrunch: Agentic AI Funding Surge in Early 2026
retrieved_docs.snippet:
TechCrunch: Agentic AI Funding Surge in Early 2026

TechCrunch reports that agentic AI startups raised over $4B in Q1 2026, with the majority of deals concentrated in orchestration infrastructure, enterprise RAG platforms, and agent evaluation tooling. Notable rounds include CrewAI's $100M Series B, a $60M raise by Composio, and continued growth at Langfuse. Investors cite the shift from single-turn chatbots to persistent, multi-step agents as the key driver of demand.

retrieved_docs.title: Five architects of the AI economy explain where the wheels are coming off
retrieved_docs.snippet:
Five architects of the AI economy explain where the wheels are coming off

Earlier this week, five people who touch every layer of the AI supply chain sat down at the Milken Global Conference in Beverly Hills, where they talked with this editor about everything from chip shortages to orbital data centers to the possibility that the whole architecture that undergirds the tech is wrong.
On stage with TechCrunch: Christophe Fouquet, CEO of ASML, the Dutch company that holds a monopoly on the extreme ultraviolet lithography machines without which modern chips would not exist; Francis deSouza, COO of Google Cloud, who is overseeing one of the biggest infrastructure bets in corporate history; Qasar Younis, co-founder and CEO of Applied Intuition, a $15 billion physical AI company that started in simulation and has since moved into defense; Dimitry Shevelenko, the chief business officer of Perplexity, the AI-native search-to-agents company; and Eve Bodnia, a quantum physicist who left academia to challenge the foundational architecture most of the AI industry takes for granted at her startup, Logical Intelligence. (Meta’s former chief AI scientist, Yan LeCun, signed on as founding chair of its technical research board earlier this year.)
